features.summary: Recent updates to Firefox's WebRender, the GPU compositor that handles how graphics are displayed on your screen, focused on improving support for different YUV formats, which are important for video processing. Specifically, the addition of **NV16 YUV format support** and fixes to the **P210 format** enhance the browser's ability to efficiently display video content, ensuring better quality and compatibility with modern web media. Changes also included refining how shapes are rendered, such as using rounded rectangles and clip masks, which enhances the visual fidelity of web graphics. These improvements contribute to smoother video playback and more visually appealing web elements, ultimately enriching the user experience.

features.summary: Recent updates to the Canvas area in Firefox enhance the performance and security of web graphics rendering. The improvements include more precise handling of quality settings for offscreen canvas blob conversions, which ensures that only valid quality values are processed, thus maintaining visual integrity. There are also adjustments to memory management and context handling that prevent information leaks and potential crashes, increasing the reliability of WebGL (Web Graphics Library) operations, which power many interactive graphics on the web. Additionally, updates aimed at optimizing code organization and compliance with coding standards promise a smoother development experience. These changes contribute to a more robust and efficient graphics framework that better serves users across various online applications and gaming experiences. **Overall, these enhancements bolster the performance and reliability of web graphics, making for a better browsing experience.**

features.summary: Recent updates to the compositing and layers area of Firefox improve the management of visual layers in the browser, particularly during shutdown, which prevents errors by not trying to restore layers that were erased. There’s also enhanced support for Android with AImageReader, facilitating better image handling for applications. A small but notable adjustment allows a greater tolerance for pixel differences when dealing with window scrolling, improving visual accuracy. Additionally, improvements in code quality—such as modernizing type usage—contribute to better performance and maintainability of the browser's architecture. Overall, these changes are aimed at enhancing the stability and visual fidelity of the Firefox browsing experience, particularly in complex rendering scenarios. **This is crucial as it ensures smoother interactions and visuals for users, especially on mobile devices.**

features.label: Image decoding

patches.author: Daniel Holbert
patches.subject: Use std::move in CopyOnWrite move-constructors, to ensure param doesn't decay into lvalue.

patches.author: Daniel Holbert
patches.subject: Migrate image/CopyOnWrite.h to use 'MakeRefPtr' instead of 'new' to allocate refcounted objects.

patches.author: Daniel Holbert
patches.subject: Pass already_AddRefed types by-value instead of by rvalue reference, in image/.

features.summary: Recent changes in the image decoding area of Firefox have focused on enhancing memory efficiency and object management during image processing. These improvements involve the use of `MakeRefPtr`, a technique that creates reference-counted objects more safely and efficiently than traditional methods. Additionally, the updated handling of parameters ensures that efficient memory practices are in place by using `std::move` and modifying the way objects are passed around, which reduces unwanted copies and improves performance. **Overall, these updates help Firefox manage images more effectively, potentially leading to faster load times and a more responsive browsing experience.**

features.label: 2D graphics (Thebes)

patches.author: Jonathan Kew

patches.subject: Always use MTA in the content process.

patches.subject: Apply letter-spacing between the two component S glyphs when es-zet is uppercased to SS.

features.summary: Recent updates to the 2D graphics processing in Firefox enhance both font rendering and performance. One significant change involves ensuring that the font family name is securely managed, which helps prevent potential issues with accessing font data, leading to more stable text rendering. Additionally, by always using the Multithreaded Apartment model (MTA) in the content process, Firefox can better utilize system resources to improve the efficiency of graphics operations. A more user-facing improvement is the precise application of letter-spacing for uppercase "SS" in the German alphabet, ensuring that the two 'S' glyphs are spaced correctly, which enhances text legibility in specific contexts. **Ultimately, these improvements contribute to a smoother and more visually appealing browsing experience.**

features.label: 2D graphics library (Moz2D)

patches.author: Jamie Nicol
patches.subject: Use biplanar IOSurface formats for 8-bit 4:2:2 video.

patches.subject: Tweak an assert to fix webdriver print tests.

features.summary: Recent updates to the 2D graphics library, Moz2D, enhance its compatibility with video formats by implementing **biplanar IOSurface formats for 8-bit 4:2:2 video**, which is important for better video playback quality on macOS systems. This change allows Firefox to handle certain types of video data more efficiently, ultimately leading to smoother viewing experiences. Additionally, some internal adjustments were made to improve the reliability of automated print tests within the WebDriver framework, ensuring that developers can trust these tests run as expected for future updates. Overall, these changes contribute to a more robust and effective multimedia experience in Firefox.

features.label: Graphics configuration

patches.author: Sotaro Ikeda
patches.subject: Enable GpuProcessAndroidImageReaderMap when pref gfx.video.aimage-reader-gpu-process-android is true

patches.author: alastor0325
patches.subject: Part 2: Record GPU adapter context on the mfcdm error event.

features.summary: Recent updates to the graphics configuration in Firefox enhance video rendering on Android devices. By enabling the **GpuProcessAndroidImageReaderMap**, the browser can better manage video inputs and outputs, leading to improved performance and potentially smoother playback. Additionally, recording the GPU adapter context during media error events allows for more effective troubleshooting and error handling, ensuring users can enjoy a more reliable video experience on their devices. This combination of improvements not only optimizes the use of graphics hardware but also strengthens overall media playback stability on Android.

features.label: Image decoders

patches.author: Jan-Niklas Jaeschke
patches.subject: Bug 2045537, part 1 - Cache the spec hash on nsIURI.

features.summary: Recent updates to Firefox's image decoders focused on improving performance and efficiency in handling images. By caching the specification hash on `nsIURI`, a component that manages URLs, Firefox can now retrieve image data more quickly when loading and rendering images on web pages. This change helps to reduce loading times and improve the overall browsing experience, especially for image-heavy sites. While the technical adjustments may not have any _immediate visible change_ for users, they contribute to a smoother and faster interaction with online content.

features.label: Canvas tests

patches.author: Erich Gubler
patches.subject: chore(angle): update `KNOWN_ON_CI` renderer list with new ANGLE value

features.summary: The recent updates to the canvas tests in Firefox involve an adjustment to the list of renderers used in the ANGLE (Almost Native Graphics Layer Engine) framework, which translates OpenGL calls to various graphics APIs. This change ensures that the most current renderer options are recognized during testing, thereby improving the reliability and accuracy of how graphical content is displayed in the browser. While this update may not result in any immediate visible changes for users, it helps maintain the browser's performance and compatibility with modern graphics standards, which is essential for rendering complex visuals on the web.

features.label: Crashtests

patches.author: Andy Leiserson
patches.subject: Flush messages before canvas readback

features.summary: Recent updates in the Firefox browser have improved the handling of **canvas readback**, which is the process of retrieving pixel data from a canvas element used in web graphics. By ensuring that messages are flushed before this readback occurs, the browser can enhance the performance and reliability of rendering tasks, minimizing potential delays or glitches when users take screenshots or engage with dynamic content. This change is significant as it ensures a smoother experience in web applications that rely heavily on graphical content, such as games and interactive media.

features.label: Media IPC

patches.author: zehuixu
patches.subject: Filter Linux WebGPU dmabuf export modifiers by EGL consumer capabilities for shared-texture swapchain.

features.summary: Recent updates to the Media IPC (Inter-Process Communication) segment of Firefox focused on enhancing the way media is handled on Linux systems using WebGPU, a technology that allows for high-performance graphics processing. Specifically, the changes involve filtering dmabuf (direct memory access buffer) export modifiers based on EGL (a platform interface for rendering OpenGL graphics) consumer capabilities, ensuring that shared textures in swapchains (a series of images to be displayed) are optimized for performance and compatibility. This improvement is significant because it can lead to better graphics rendering quality and smoother media playback for users on Linux, ensuring a more consistent and engaging experience when viewing web content.

features.label: WebGPU

patches.author: Keith Cirkel
patches.subject: Part 55 - Fixup final classes using _FINAL cycle collecting isupports macro in dom/webgpu

features.summary: Recent updates to the WebGPU area of Firefox focused on improving memory management by fixing issues related to the cycle collecting of final classes that use the _FINAL macro. WebGPU is a modern graphics API designed to bring high-performance graphics and computing to web applications, enabling developers to create more dynamic and visually rich experiences. By enhancing memory management, these changes help ensure more efficient use of system resources, which ultimately leads to smoother performance when running intensive graphics applications in Firefox. This is particularly important for applications that rely on fast rendering and processing, such as video games and interactive visualizations.

features.label: WebGPU backend (wgpu)

patches.author: Teodor Tanasoaia
patches.subject: Enable `InstanceFlags::STRICT_WEBGPU_COMPLIANCE`.

features.summary: Recent updates to the Firefox browser's WebGPU backend have introduced a feature called `InstanceFlags::STRICT_WEBGPU_COMPLIANCE`. This change enhances the compatibility of WebGPU, a modern graphics API used for rendering graphics and performing computations on the web, by ensuring it adheres more closely to the official specifications. **This means developers can expect their applications to behave more consistently across different browsers, improving performance and reliability in web-based graphics applications.** This change is particularly important as the use of WebGPU becomes more common in high-performance web apps, games, and visualizations, allowing for richer user experiences.

features.label: WebGPU tests

patches.author: Erich Gubler
patches.subject: chore(webgpu): update CTS to cf0073691abc3b97587411c105e14f0af795db37

features.summary: Recent updates to the WebGPU tests included an important refresh of the Conformance Test Suite (CTS) to a newer version. WebGPU is a web standard that allows developers to take advantage of the GPU for high-performance graphics and computations in web applications, similar to technologies like WebGL but with enhanced capabilities. This update helps ensure that Firefox remains compliant with the latest WebGPU specifications, which is crucial for developers to create rich and efficient web experiences. While users may not see immediate changes, these updates play a vital role in maintaining Firefox's competitiveness and performance in rendering complex graphics.

features.summary: Recent changes to WebRender, which is Firefox's graphics rendering engine that uses the GPU, have significantly enhanced its support for various YUV formats, especially important for video processing. With the addition of support for NV16 YUV format—now renamed to P210—and fixes for other formats, **WebRender can better handle video content, improving the experience for users during video playback and streaming**. Additionally, the updates streamline how shapes like rounded rectangles are rendered and introduce more efficient processes for applying visual effects, thereby making the browser's rendering performance faster and more efficient. The overall focus on optimizing graphical performance and supporting advanced image formats means users will see better visual fidelity and smoother animations while browsing.

features.summary: Recent updates to the **compositing and layering** system in Firefox focused on improving the performance and reliability of rendering, especially for touch interactions and visuals on different platforms like Android and Wayland. One significant enhancement is the introduction of **AImageReader support on Android**, which allows for better handling of images during rendering tasks, making the browser smoother and more responsive. Additionally, improvements in the handling of touch events, such as refining the processing of scrolling and touch movements, aim to provide a more fluid user experience by reducing delays when interacting with content. These changes not only streamline the way layers are managed but also ensure that the visuals on screen are rendered efficiently, contributing to a quicker and more seamless browsing experience.

features.summary: Recent updates in the 2D graphics area of Firefox have focused on improving font handling and performance. These changes include better management of font entries through enhanced reference counting, which helps prevent errors related to memory allocation. A significant adjustment involves applying **letter-spacing for uppercase transformations of the es-zet character**, ensuring proper typographic representation. Additionally, optimizations around text processing have been made, including the ability to skip unnecessary checks for simple Latin text, leading to faster rendering and an overall smoother browsing experience. These enhancements collectively contribute to a more efficient and visually appealing presentation of text on websites.

features.summary: Recent updates to the Canvas area of Firefox have primarily improved the handling of WebGL (a web technology for rendering 3D graphics) and offscreen canvas functionality. A more robust management of memory and resources is now in place, including changes like ensuring specific types of canvas surfaces and better adherence to WebGL specifications. Additionally, **the updates enhance the performance and reliability of converting and recording canvas graphics**, ensuring that quality settings are properly enforced and memory leaks are minimized. These changes not only streamline operations but also aim to improve the overall experience for developers and users engaging with graphics-rich web applications.

features.label: Graphics configuration

patches.author: Sotaro Ikeda
patches.subject: Enable GpuProcessAndroidImageReaderMap when pref gfx.video.aimage-reader-gpu-process-android is true

patches.author: alastor0325
patches.subject: Part 2: Record GPU adapter context on the mfcdm error event.

features.summary: Recent updates to the graphics configuration in Firefox enhance video rendering on Android devices. By enabling the **GpuProcessAndroidImageReaderMap**, the browser can better manage video inputs and outputs, leading to improved performance and potentially smoother playback. Additionally, recording the GPU adapter context during media error events allows for more effective troubleshooting and error handling, ensuring users can enjoy a more reliable video experience on their devices. This combination of improvements not only optimizes the use of graphics hardware but also strengthens overall media playback stability on Android.

features.label: Graphics tests

patches.author: Lee Salzman
patches.subject: Ensure DrawTargetSkia::CanCreateSimilarDrawTarget checks buffer size.

patches.author: Hiroyuki Ikezoe
patches.subject: Use PuppetWidget for window less browser even on headless mode.

features.summary: Recent updates in the graphics tests area have improved how Firefox handles rendering targets. The change ensures that the **DrawTargetSkia** component can accurately check if it can create similar drawing surfaces based on the buffer size, enhancing performance and efficiency in rendering graphics. Additionally, the use of **PuppetWidget** for handling window-less operations, even in headless mode (where the browser runs without a user interface), allows for more consistent testing behavior. These updates matter as they contribute to better graphics handling and reliability within the browser, ensuring a smoother experience for users dealing with various visual content.

features.label: Crashtests

patches.author: Andy Leiserson
patches.subject: Flush messages before canvas readback

features.summary: Recent updates in the Firefox browser have improved the handling of **canvas readback**, which is the process of retrieving pixel data from a canvas element used in web graphics. By ensuring that messages are flushed before this readback occurs, the browser can enhance the performance and reliability of rendering tasks, minimizing potential delays or glitches when users take screenshots or engage with dynamic content. This change is significant as it ensures a smoother experience in web applications that rely heavily on graphical content, such as games and interactive media.

features.label: Docs

patches.author: Sylvestre Ledru
patches.subject: Convert gfx/docs RST documentation to Markdown

features.summary: Recent updates in the documentation area of Firefox involved converting the existing RST (reStructuredText) documents to Markdown format. Markdown is a simpler and more widely used markup language that makes the documentation easier to read and edit, which is beneficial for developers and contributors who want to understand or improve the browser. This change is significant because it enhances accessibility and streamlines the documentation process, facilitating better collaboration within the development community. _Users may not notice immediate changes_, but this improvement in the underlying documentation will support future features and fixes more efficiently.

features.label: Graphics core

patches.author: Bas Schouten
patches.subject: Remove legacy mirrors for a large set of probes primarily used for performance monitoring.

features.summary: Recent updates to the graphics core of Firefox involved the **removal of legacy mirrors for a large set of performance monitoring probes**. These probes are tools used to gather data on how well the browser performs, specifically in rendering graphics. By eliminating outdated components, the Firefox team aims to streamline performance monitoring and focus on more modern, efficient methods, ultimately leading to better optimization and resource use in the browser's graphics handling.

features.label: Media IPC

patches.author: zehuixu
patches.subject: Filter Linux WebGPU dmabuf export modifiers by EGL consumer capabilities for shared-texture swapchain.

features.summary: Recent updates to the Media IPC (Inter-Process Communication) segment of Firefox focused on enhancing the way media is handled on Linux systems using WebGPU, a technology that allows for high-performance graphics processing. Specifically, the changes involve filtering dmabuf (direct memory access buffer) export modifiers based on EGL (a platform interface for rendering OpenGL graphics) consumer capabilities, ensuring that shared textures in swapchains (a series of images to be displayed) are optimized for performance and compatibility. This improvement is significant because it can lead to better graphics rendering quality and smoother media playback for users on Linux, ensuring a more consistent and engaging experience when viewing web content.

features.label: WebGPU

patches.author: Keith Cirkel
patches.subject: Part 55 - Fixup final classes using _FINAL cycle collecting isupports macro in dom/webgpu

features.summary: Recent updates to the WebGPU area of Firefox focused on improving memory management by fixing issues related to the cycle collecting of final classes that use the _FINAL macro. WebGPU is a modern graphics API designed to bring high-performance graphics and computing to web applications, enabling developers to create more dynamic and visually rich experiences. By enhancing memory management, these changes help ensure more efficient use of system resources, which ultimately leads to smoother performance when running intensive graphics applications in Firefox. This is particularly important for applications that rely on fast rendering and processing, such as video games and interactive visualizations.
